name: Antelope Springs
short_description: BLM rockhounding site in Juab County featuring agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. Personal use collecting allowed with no permit required.
description:
Antelope Springs is a public BLM rockhounding location in central Utah's Juab County, offering accessible collecting opportunities for agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz. The site sits within Utah's Basin and Range province, where Cenozoic volcanic activity and hydrothermal processes created abundant microcrystalline silicate deposits now weathering from hillside outcrops and wash gravels. Rockhounders typically find small to medium specimens in various colors, with banded agates and red-orange jasper being particularly common in the local drainage systems.

The Antelope Springs area is characterized by semi-arid desert terrain with scattered sagebrush and low vegetation, making it accessible during cooler months. The landscape includes gentle to moderate slopes with scattered rock outcrops and wash bottoms where specimens weather out naturally. This location is popular with both beginning and experienced rockhounders due to its easy access and consistent specimen recovery.

Collecting here follows standard BLM recreational guidelines with no permits required. Visitors should respect the 25 lb per day personal use limit and avoid any areas showing signs of previous commercial extraction or mining claims. The open terrain allows for systematic searching along washes and hillside exposures where specimens are most concentrated.
history: This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Cenozoic volcanic activity, particularly during the Eocene to Miocene epochs, deposited silica-rich fluids and ashfall deposits that subsequently crystallized into agate, chalcedony, and jasper nodules. Natural weathering and erosion over millions of years has exposed these secondary silicate minerals in accessible wash gravels and hillside outcrops, making the area attractive to modern rockhounders.
tips: Search along the bottoms and banks of washes and drainages where water erosion has concentrated specimens over time - this is where the best material weathering from hillside sources collects. Use a magnifying glass to examine matrix material for chalcedony and agate banding; reddish and orange-colored specimens are most abundant here. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and a lightweight digging tool to test promising-looking gravels, and always fill holes back in after searching. Early morning or late afternoon light helps spot translucent agate specimens glinting in the sun.
terrain_notes: Rolling desert terrain at approximately 5,500 feet elevation with gentle to moderate slopes. Scattered sagebrush and sparse vegetation typical of Utah's semi-arid interior. Walking distances from parking areas typically range from immediate access to 0.5 mile hikes to productive hillsides. Terrain is rocky with firm footing on most areas, though loose material is common in washes. No significant elevation gain required for typical collecting areas.
best_season: October - April. The region experiences hot, dry summers with temperatures frequently exceeding 85 degrees Fahrenheit, making spring and fall the most comfortable collecting times. Winter is possible but occasional snow may limit access to higher areas. Late fall (October-November) and early spring (March-April) offer ideal conditions with mild temperatures and good specimen visibility after seasonal rains.
written_directions: From Nephi (approximately 15 miles west), take US 50 east toward US 6. Continue east on US 6 for approximately 8 miles. Look for BLM access roads heading south or north from the highway near Antelope Springs area mile markers. Local county roads and dirt tracks provide access to collection areas; high-clearance vehicles are recommended but not always required. Consult current BLM Utah maps or contact the Richfield BLM Field Office for specific access road conditions before visiting, as seasonal closures may affect approach routes.
road_conditions: Dirt and graded gravel access roads, generally passable with passenger vehicles during dry conditions. Seasonal washboard present. High-clearance recommended but not required for main access areas.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along access roads. No developed parking lot; park safely off roadways. Space for 2-4 vehicles at typical access points.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation. Collect only loose surface material; do not break rock formations or blast. Respect any posted mining claims. Fill in holes after searching. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No breaking of in-place rock formations
Respect posted mining claims
Fill holes after searching
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Nephi, approximately 15 miles west, has gas stations, restaurants, and basic supplies. Mona, closer at 8 miles northwest, has limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved roads to parking and collection areas. Terrain is rocky but stable footing. Short walks of 0.5 mile or less typically required to reach productive collecting spots. Sturdy footwear recommended. Not suitable for wheelchairs due to rough road and terrain, but accessible to people with moderate mobility.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Antelope Springs in Utah?
faq.answer: Antelope Springs is known for banded agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. Red-orange jasper and patterned agates are the most commonly found materials. Specimens range from small to medium size, with many pieces suitable for cabochon cutting or display.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding at Antelope Springs BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Antelope Springs. The site is on public BLM land where personal use collection is allowed under FLPMA guidelines. You must limit your collection to 25 lbs per day and follow standard BLM rules regarding surface collection only.

faq.question: What is the best time to go rockhounding near Antelope Springs, Juab County?
faq.answer: October through April offers the most comfortable collecting conditions, with mild temperatures and good specimen visibility. Avoid the hot summer months when temperatures exceed 85 degrees. Late fall (October-November) and early spring (March-April) provide ideal conditions with cool weather and seasonal moisture that helps expose new specimens.

faq.question: How do I get to Antelope Springs from Nephi, Utah for rockhounding?
faq.answer: From Nephi, take US 50 east toward US 6, then continue east on US 6 for approximately 8 miles. Look for BLM access roads heading north or south near the Antelope Springs area. Access roads are unpaved gravel tracks; high-clearance vehicles are recommended but passenger vehicles can access main collecting areas during dry conditions.

faq.question: Is Antelope Springs rockhounding site good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Antelope Springs is very beginner-friendly and ideal for families with children ages 6 and up. The terrain is relatively flat and accessible, specimens weather naturally into washes, and no special equipment is required beyond basic collecting tools. Kids enjoy finding colorful agate and jasper pieces, making it a rewarding introduction to rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Antelope Springs?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket, small digging tool or geology hammer, magnifying glass for examining specimens, sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, and water. High-clearance vehicle access is helpful but not always required. A backpack or bag for carrying your 25 lb daily limit is essential, along with a map showing BLM boundaries to ensure you stay on public land.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Antelope Springs, Utah. No permit required on public BLM land. Open rockhounding with 25 lb daily limit.
